issues.title: Welcome to The Clearing
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: Welcome to The Clearing
issues.body:
If you are reading this, something is wrong with the way you are working.

Not wrong like bad engineer. Wrong like something changed in the last 18 months that nobody warned you about.

You are shipping more than ever. Your velocity metrics have never looked better. And yet:

You cannot quite remember the last time you wrote a function from scratch. You cannot quite explain why that code you accepted last Tuesday actually works. You are learning less and producing more and feeling exactly none of the satisfaction that either of those things used to bring.

We call it AI fatigue. You are not burned out. You are not lazy. You are not struggling with change. Something real is happening to the craft, and you deserve a name for it.

Why we built this

Three of us Sunny, Anny, and Dragon Hunter are engineers who have been watching our friends go through this. Good engineers. Serious engineers. People who care about what they do.

The symptoms kept showing up the same way: Sunday dread that does not match the actual difficulty of Monday tasks. A creeping sense that the work is not yours. A vague guilt about being ungrateful for superpowers that do not feel super.

We looked for resources and found productivity content. Nothing honest about what it feels like to slowly stop recognizing your own skills. Nothing about how to protect the craft.

So we built The Clearing.

What we are offering

A free 5-question AI Fatigue Quiz that tells you where you are from using AI mindfully to considering leaving tech.

A plain-English explainer of what is actually happening to your brain when AI writes your code for you.

A 30-day recovery plan built from research, not hustle culture.

And a weekly letter this one with one honest thing per week. No growth hacking. No master the future. Just: this is what is happening, and here is one thing that actually helps.

One thing you can do today

Answer one question honestly:

Could you build your current project core feature from scratch no AI, no reference, no Stack Overflow in the time it would take you to prompt for it?

If yes you are okay. Keep paying attention.

If no you are in the right place. This letter is for you.

Start with the quiz. It will take 2 minutes and you will know exactly where you stand.

Take the Quiz

issues.title: What 2,000 engineers told us about AI fatigue
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: What 2,000 engineers told us about AI fatigue
issues.body:
Three engineers started The Clearing after watching the same thing happen to different friends:

I feel like I am shipping code but not writing it. I feel like I am learning but not growing. I feel like I should be grateful for AI tools and I am just tired.

That unnamed exhaustion had a name: AI fatigue.

We built clearing-ai.com a free quiz and recovery guide for engineers experiencing it.

Here is what 2,000+ quiz takers told us:

63%
feel like middlemen between AI output and actual work
58%
have noticed measurable skill changes in themselves
71%
took the quiz because something concrete happened
44%
are considering leaving tech entirely because of AI fatigue

These are not weak signals. They are a pattern.

What AI fatigue actually is

It is not burnout. Burnout is from too much work.

AI fatigue is from the wrong kind of work work that looks like productivity and feels like erasure. Work where you cannot trace your decisions back to your own thinking.

One thing you can do this week

Track the gap. For one week: notice when AI handles something you used to handle. Write it down. By Friday, count how many gaps you have logged. The number is data. It is not guilt it is information about what AI has quietly taken over.

Take the quiz and get your personalized tier breakdown.

Take the Quiz

issues.title: The Sunday scaries do not stop. Here is why.
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Sunday scaries do not stop. Here is why.
issues.body:
Something keeps showing up in the messages you send us:

I took a weekend off. I felt fine. Monday morning, the dread came back like it never left.

That is not burnout. Or rather it is not just burnout.

Burnout from overwork typically improves with rest. You push less, you recover. The mechanism is depletion.

AI fatigue works differently. It is not depletion it is displacement. You have not been doing too much work. You have been doing the wrong kind of work. And rest does not restore something you were not doing in the first place.

Why the Sunday scaries do not stop

The hollow productivity loop. You shipped all week. You closed tickets. You shipped features. And yet: you have the vague, uncomfortable sense that you did not actually build anything.

This is not imposter syndrome. Imposter syndrome is about feeling like a fraud despite competence. This is different it is about noticing that the connection between your effort and your skill has been severed.

Your Sundays know the difference even when your weekdays pretended not to notice.

What to do about it

You need experiences of actual building, not just output shipping.

The gap between what you produce with AI and what you could produce without it that is the skill displacement. You cannot fix it with rest. You have to rebuild it with practice.

One no-AI hour per week is where most people start.

The 30-day plan on the site has a specific week for rebuilding the Sunday feeling.

Start the 30-Day Plan

issues.title: The one tactic that actually works (and nobody is doing it)
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The one tactic that actually works (and nobody is doing it)
issues.body:
Week three. If you have been following the 30-day plan, you are somewhere between started to see the shape of the problem and slowly remembering why you got into this in the first place.

Today I want to talk about the one recovery tactic that shows up most consistently in our quiz data across all four tiers, all experience levels, all stack types as actually working. And almost nobody is doing it.

The Explanation Requirement

Here is the practice: Before you accept any AI-generated code as final, you have to be able to explain it out loud, to no one in particular, as if you were teaching someone.

Not reading the code. Not glancing at the comments. Explaining it.

So this function takes the user ID, then it queries the database to get their preferences, and then it merges those with the default settings because the query uses a LEFT JOIN...

The act of explaining reveals gaps you were not aware of. It forces you to confront whether you actually understand what the code does not just that it works. It is uncomfortable. That is the point.

Why It Works (The Research)

Psychologists call this the retrieval practice effect. Robert Bjork desirable difficulty research shows that the act of trying to retrieve information not just re-reading it dramatically improves long-term retention and understanding.

When you force yourself to explain code you did not write, you are doing retrieval practice against code you should have written. The gap between what you can explain and what you cannot is exactly the gap where learning used to happen.

AI did not break your brain. It just made it very easy to skip the part where understanding gets built.

Two Weeks of The Explanation Requirement

Try it for two weeks. Every piece of AI code you accept, run it through this filter:

  • Can I explain what this does in 60 seconds?
  • Could I explain the trade-offs in this implementation?
  • Would I have solved it this way? Why or why not?

Track how often you can answer yes to all three. That number week over week is a real measurement of whether you are still in the learning loop.

Where are you in the 30-day plan? Reply and tell me which week you are on and what has surprised you.

Download the 30-Day Checklist

issues.title: The Sunday Night Reckoning
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Sunday Night Reckoning
issues.body:
There is a specific kind of Sunday night that engineers know well.

It shows up around 9 PM. The week work is done technically. You shipped. You closed tickets. You responded to the review comments.

But something feels off. You cannot quite name it. You tell yourself it is the weekend ending. The Monday coming. The usual.

But it is not the weekend ending.

It is the specific feeling of having spent another week doing things you did not fully understand. Solving problems you did not actually solve. Shipping code you could not write from scratch.

You are not tired. You are hollow.

What AI Fatigue Actually Feels Like

  • Sunday dread that does not match the actual workload. The work was easy. The dread was real.
  • Relief when code works without knowing why. It compiled. Ship it. followed by a vague sense of fraud.
  • Skills that used to be automatic. You used to know how to debug that. Now you prompt for it.
  • A gap between what you shipped and what you built. The code is yours but the work was done by someone else.

This is not burnout. Burnout is from too much work. This is from the wrong kind of work work that looks like productivity and feels like erasure.

One Practice That Helps

Weekly I built this without AI sessions.

Once a week same day, same time solve a real problem using only your own knowledge. No copilots. No ChatGPT. No code completion.

The first session is usually uncomfortable. The second is a little easier. By the fourth, something starts to come back.

It is not about the skill. It is about the feeling. The feeling of I made this.

If you took the quiz, your results page has a personalized recovery plan. If you have not, it takes 3 minutes:

Take the AI Fatigue Quiz

What AI Fatigue Actually Feels Like

Most content about AI fatigue focuses on productivity. Are you using AI tools efficiently? Are you maintaining your skills?

That is the wrong frame.

The real symptom is subtler. Sunday dread that does not match the actual workload. Relief when code works without knowing why. Skills that used to be automatic that you now prompt for. A growing gap between what you shipped and what you built.

This is not burnout. This is from the wrong kind of work work that looks like productivity and feels like erasure.

One Practice That Helps

Weekly I built this without AI sessions.

Once a week same day, same time solve a real problem using only your own knowledge. No copilots. No ChatGPT.

Most-read page this week: The Identity Crisis at the Heart of Senior Engineer AI Fatigue

Read: Senior Identity

issues.title: The Debugger Drift
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The one skill AI is quietly destroying
issues.body:
Two weeks ago, a senior engineer told me something that stuck with me.

I can build anything now. I ship more code than I ever have. But when something breaks at 2am really breaks I have no idea what is happening. I just prompt-storm until something sticks.

He has 12 years of experience. He is not alone.

This is what we are calling The Debugger Drift the slow erosion of the skill that separates engineers from code assemblers.

The paradox nobody talks about

AI makes you faster at writing code. It makes you slower at understanding it.

Every time AI handles a tricky bug, you learn that a tricky bug has a solution. But you do not learn how the solution was found. You learn the answer. You lose the diagnosis.

AI-generated code is optimistically written. It assumes the happy path. It does not know about the edge case your team discovered in production at 4pm on a Friday three months ago. So when something breaks, the AI explanation is often plausible but incomplete.

And you cannot tell the difference.

Why this hurts seniors most

There is a counterintuitive finding from cognitive science: the more expertise you have, the more you rely on context years of patterns, failure modes, edge cases you have seen. AI does not just speed up your work. It strips away the contextual scaffolding that your expertise rests on.

The result: senior engineers often feel the drift more acutely than juniors. You know something is off. You cannot quite name it.

The Explanation Requirement

Before you accept any AI suggestion explain it out loud. In your own words. As if you are teaching it to a colleague.

I fixed the null pointer by adding a guard clause because...

I refactored this loop because when the list is empty it would...

If you cannot explain it in a complete sentence you are not done debugging. The AI bought you a plausible answer, not understanding.

The concrete test

Next time something breaks and AI gives you a fix: before you apply it, ask yourself do I know why this fixes it?

If the answer is it just does you are debugging with a blindfold on.

This week most-shared story: the engineer who realized he could not remember the last time he had debugged something for more than 10 minutes without reaching for AI. His solution: one hour per week, no AI assistance.

Read: The Debugger Drift

issues.title: The Skill You Stopped Practicing
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Skill You Stopped Practicing
issues.body:
Here is a question you can answer in 10 seconds:

What coding skill do you practice less now than you did two years ago?

Not what is harder or what is worse specifically: what did you used to do with your own hands that AI now does faster?

Most engineers have an answer within 3 seconds. They just do not like what it is.

The Compounding Invisible

The skill loss does not announce itself. There is no moment where you sit down to write a function and suddenly realize you cannot. It happens in the gaps the moments where AI handles something and you move on without noticing what you just stopped learning.

You do not see the learning you are not getting. You only eventually notice the understanding that did not accumulate.

Here is the part that makes it worse: you usually cannot measure the decline, because you were not measuring the skill before. You just knew how to do things. Now you know how to prompt. These feel similar in the moment. They are not similar over 18 months.

The Three Most Common Losses

Debugging. The most cited loss. When AI writes most of your code, you debug less. When you debug less, you read less of your own code in detail. When you read less, you understand less. This compounds until something breaks and you read your own code like someone else for the first time.

Architecture thinking. It requires sitting with ambiguity long enough to see the shape of a solution. AI offers a shape immediately. Taking it means you stop sitting with the ambiguity. The muscle atrophies without use.

Reading unfamiliar code. Code written by others becomes harder to parse. Not because you are less capable because you stopped practicing the translation work.

The Practice Gap Triage

Three questions:

  1. What did I used to be able to do in 20 minutes that now takes me 45 minutes without AI? That is your skill gap. Start there.
  2. What do I avoid looking at carefully because AI generated most of it? That is your confidence gap. Start there.
  3. What decision would I make differently if I understood this system better? That is your understanding gap. That is the expensive one.

Take the AI Fatigue Quiz it helps identify your specific profile and the gaps most likely to affect you.

Take the Quiz

issues.title: The Sunday Night Reckoning
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Sunday Night Reckoning
issues.body:
Sunday evening. You are not doing anything wrong. But something feels heavy.

Maybe it is the ambient awareness that tomorrow starts again. The pile does not care that it is Monday. The Slack notifications do not pause for the weekend. And lately, there is something else a specific flavor of dread that did not used to be there.

We are calling it The Sunday Night Reckoning.

What is different now

Five years ago, Sunday dread was mostly about the inbox and the sprint. Work. Manageable.

Now there is a second layer that was not there before:

You are not just starting work. You are starting a performance.

The performance of keeping up with every new tool, every model update, every colleague who seems to be shipping twice as fast. And here is the part nobody names out loud: you are performing to stay current, but you are not sure you are actually getting better. The velocity feels borrowed. The knowledge feels thin.

The Sunday Night Reckoning is not just I do not want to work. It is I do not know if I can keep up and feel like myself.

The Tuesday Morning Test

When you finish a good coding session do you feel the same satisfaction on Sunday night that you did three years ago?

Not are you productive. Do you feel the satisfaction of having been the engineer who built it?

If that answer has changed that is not personal failure. That is a structural problem with how AI tooling interacts with the conditions that make engineering meaningful.

One thing to do this week

Monday: before you open a single AI tool, spend 20 minutes building something from scratch.

It can be a side project. A refactor of something you care about. A personal utility. Anything. No Copilot. No Claude. No ChatGPT. Just you and a blank editor.

You might feel slow. You might feel rusty. You might feel like you are wasting time because AI could do this in 5 minutes.

That is exactly the point.

If you have taken a weekend and still feel wrecked by Sunday evening:

Read: Why Resting Does Not Fix AI Fatigue

issues.title: The Sunday Reckoning
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Sunday Night Reckoning
issues.body:
There is a particular kind of Sunday that engineers know well.

You spend the day trying to rest. You go for a walk. You read something. You have dinner. And then, usually around 6 or 7pm, it arrives: the feeling.

Tomorrow is Monday. And you are not ready. Not because you did not work hard enough this week. Because the week ahead looks like another week of more tools, more prompts, more context to maintain, more output to curate, more AI decisions to review.

You are not burned out on work. You are burned up on a specific kind of exhaustion that the industry has not named.

This is the Sunday reckoning.

Why Sundays hit differently for AI-fatigued engineers

The Sunday problem is not new. Engineers have always had some version of the pre-Monday weight.

What AI changed is the texture of that weight.

Before AI tools, the Sunday dread had a specific flavor: Tomorrow I have to be smart again. There was a pressure to perform, to demonstrate competence.

With AI tools, the Sunday dread has a different flavor: Tomorrow I am going to spend the day reviewing someone else thinking. The pressure is not to perform it is to curate.

The dread is not about being inadequate. It is about another week of being a middleman.

Three things worth trying this Sunday

1. A Saturday evening reset ritual. Move one intentional action to Saturday evening: a short walk, a specific time with no screens, a few minutes writing down the one thing you are actually looking forward to building this week. The goal is not productivity. It is to get ahead of the dread before it compounds overnight.

2. Define one thing you will do without AI this week. Not a whole day. Just one thing a feature, a bug, a piece of a feature where the goal is to see what you can figure out without prompting anything. This is not about proving a point. It is about keeping the thread alive.

3. Sunday evening: Write one honest sentence. Something like: I shipped more than I understood this week. Or: I could not explain half the code I approved. That sentence is data.

We are collecting more stories this week if you have noticed your debugging instincts changing, we would love to hear it.

Share Your Story

issues.title: The Middleman Problem
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Middleman Problem
issues.body:
Here is something that is showing up constantly in the conversations we have with engineers who take the AI Fatigue Quiz:

You are shipping more than ever. But you feel like less.

Not imposter syndrome. Not burnout. Something more specific: the middleman problem.

What the middleman problem actually is

In traditional software engineering, there was a direct line between you and the work.

You understood the problem. You designed the solution. You wrote the code. You shipped it. You could trace every decision back to a conversation you had, a trade-off you made, a mistake you learned from.

That line is broken now.

You understand the problem. AI generates the solution. You review it. You ship it. The outcome is your responsibility but the thinking is not fully yours. You are vouching for work you did not fully generate.

This creates a particular kind of exhaustion. Not from hard work. From responsibility without understanding.

Why just be more intentional does not fix it

The obvious advice is: review the AI output more carefully, understand what it is doing, make sure you are learning from it.

This is good advice. It is also incomplete.

The problem is not that you are not paying attention. It is that the feedback loop that used to create understanding the struggle, the debugging, the why did that break has been moved upstream into the AI process, not yours.

You cannot retrieve what you did not generate.

The Author Test

When you look at a feature you shipped last week how much of it could you explain from memory? Not the structure. The reasoning. Why that approach and not another one. What the trade-offs were.

If the answer is less than I would like, that is not a character flaw. That is a signal.

We wrote a longer breakdown of the difference between imposter syndrome and AI fatigue at the site because getting the diagnosis right is the first step toward actually fixing it.

Read: Imposter Syndrome vs AI Fatigue

issues.title: The Middleman Problem
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Middleman Problem
issues.body:
Last week, we asked engineers a simple question:

When you ship code that an AI wrote how much of it could you reproduce from scratch right now?

The answers were illuminating.

71% said: less than half.

44% said: almost none.

These are not junior engineers. These are experienced developers who are paying close attention to what AI tooling has quietly done to their relationship with their own work.

This is the middleman problem.

What the middleman problem actually is

A middleman evaluates output. They do not create it. They approve it or reject it based on criteria they understand.

When you spend 18 months as an AI-assisted engineer, something quiet happens: you get very good at evaluating AI-generated code. What you might lose slowly enough not to notice until it is significant is the ability to generate the original.

Not because you got dumber. Because the generative work atrophied from disuse.

This is different from imposter syndrome. Imposter syndrome is about perception distorted by anxiety. The middleman problem is functional. Something has actually changed about what you can do.

Why it is hard to address

The middleman problem is invisible from the outside.

Your velocity looks fine. Your output looks fine. Your performance review is fine. The gap between you with AI and you without AI grows so slowly that you do not notice until someone asks: Could you build this without AI tools?

And you realize you do not know.

One practice that actually helps

The Explanation Requirement.

Before accepting any AI suggestion, complete this sentence out loud:

I added this because...

Not the AI added this because. You. Why is this the right approach? What would break if you removed it?

If you are in the middleman problem you are not alone, and it is not permanent. The 30-day plan has a specific week for rebuilding.

Start the 30-Day Recovery Plan

issues.body:
There is a particular kind of Sunday that engineers know well.

You spend the day trying to rest. You go for a walk. You read something. You have dinner. And then, usually around 6 or 7pm, it arrives: the feeling.

Tomorrow is Monday. And you are not ready. Not because you did not work hard enough this week. Because the week ahead looks like another week of more tools, more prompts, more context to maintain, more output to curate, more AI decisions to review.

You are not burned out on work. You are burned up on a specific kind of exhaustion that the industry has not named.

This is the Sunday reckoning.

Why Sundays hit differently for AI-fatigued engineers

The Sunday problem is not new. Engineers have always had some version of the pre-Monday weight the awareness that tomorrow problems are waiting.

What AI changed is the texture of that weight.

Before AI tools, the Sunday dread had a specific flavor: Tomorrow I have to be smart again. There was a pressure to perform, to demonstrate competence.

With AI tools, the Sunday dread has a different flavor: Tomorrow I am going to spend the day reviewing someone else thinking. The pressure is not to perform it is to curate.

Three things worth trying this Sunday

1. A Saturday evening reset ritual. Move one intentional action to Saturday evening: a short walk, a specific time with no screens, a few minutes writing down the one thing you are actually looking forward to building this week. The goal is not productivity. It is to get ahead of the dread before it compounds overnight.

2. Define one thing you will do without AI this week. Not a whole day. Not a big commitment. Just one thing a feature, a bug, a piece of a feature where the goal is to see what you can figure out without prompting anything. This is not about proving a point. It is about keeping the thread alive.

3. Sunday evening: Write one honest sentence. Not a plan. Not a productivity note. Just one honest sentence about how you are actually showing up to work right now. Something like: I shipped more than I understood this week. Get it out of your head and onto paper. That sentence is data.

We are collecting more stories this week if you have noticed something changing, we would love to hear it.

Share Your Story

issues.title: The Explanation Requirement
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Explanation Requirement
issues.body:
There is a test you can run on your own AI usage right now.

Next time you accept an AI suggestion in your code editor before you accept it, before you review it, before you even read it carefully stop and complete this sentence out loud:

I added this because...

Not the AI added this because. You. Why is this the right approach? Why this pattern instead of another? What problem does it solve that you could not have solved yourself in the time it took to prompt?

If you can finish the sentence: the code is becoming yours.

If you cannot if the suggestion arrives, you understand that it works, and you could not have generated it yourself in the same time you have not learned the code. You have just received an answer.

This is the Explanation Requirement.

Why it matters more than you think

The goal is not to refuse AI suggestions. The goal is to use them as learning moments rather than output-delivery mechanisms.

Here is the problem: when you accept an AI suggestion you do not understand, three things happen simultaneously:

1. You ship code you cannot reproduce

2. You miss the learning opportunity that the problem would have provided

3. The gap between working code and your understanding grows invisibly

This happens slowly enough that you do not notice until something breaks in that area three weeks later and you read your own code like someone else.

The 30-second rule

You do not need to interrogate every line. You need to complete one honest sentence.

I added this because...

If you cannot do it in 30 seconds that is data. It tells you this suggestion lives in a gap in your understanding.

The weekly calibration

Once a week Friday afternoon works well review your week PRs. For each significant change, ask: Could I explain why this approach was taken without referencing the code?

If yes: the work is becoming yours.

If no: you have had another week of output without learning.

The Clearing is full of practices like this ways to use AI without having AI use your learning loop. Browse the recovery guides or start the 30-day checklist.

Start the 30-Day Recovery Plan

issues.title: The Skill You Stopped Practicing
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Skill You Stopped Practicing
issues.body:
Here is a question you can answer in 10 seconds:

What coding skill do you practice less now than you did two years ago?

Not what is harder or what is worse specifically: what did you used to do with your own hands that AI now does faster?

Most engineers have an answer within 3 seconds. They just do not like what it is.

The Compounding Invisible

The skill loss does not announce itself. There is no moment where you sit down to write a function and suddenly realize you cannot. It happens in the gaps the moments where AI handles something and you move on without noticing what you just stopped learning.

This is the compounding invisible. You do not see the learning you are not getting. You only eventually notice the understanding that did not accumulate.

Here is the part that makes it worse: you usually cannot measure the decline, because you were not measuring the skill before. You just knew how to do things. Now you know how to prompt. These feel similar in the moment. They are not similar over 18 months.

The Three Most Common Losses

Debugging. The most cited loss. When AI writes most of your code, you debug less. When you debug less, you read less of your own code in detail. When you read less, you understand less. This compounds until something breaks in a familiar area and you read your own code like someone else for the first time.

Architecture thinking. It requires sitting with ambiguity long enough to see the shape of a solution. AI offers a shape immediately. Taking it means you stop sitting with the ambiguity. Taking it enough times means the ambiguity stays not because you chose it, but because the muscle atrophied without use.

Reading unfamiliar code. Code written by others, in unfamiliar styles or older patterns, becomes harder to parse. Not because you are less capable because you stopped practicing the translation work. You were good at it once. You have not done it in a while.

What Two Years Actually Looks Like

Here is the math no one runs:

If AI assists on 70% of your coding tasks, and you learn something from each task you do manually, you have gone from roughly 200 learning events per week to roughly 60. Over 2 years: roughly 14,500 fewer learning moments embedded in your work.

This is not failure. It is just arithmetic.

The question is not whether this happened. It is what you want to do about it.

The Practice Gap Triage

Three questions:

  1. What did I used to be able to do in 20 minutes that now takes me 45 minutes without AI? That is your skill gap. Start there.
  2. What do I avoid looking at carefully because AI generated most of it? That is your confidence gap. Start there.
  3. What decision would I make differently if I understood this system better? That is your understanding gap. That is the expensive one.

Try the Explanation Requirement before accepting any AI suggestion, complete the sentence I added this because. If you cannot, that is your skill gap. That is where to put the attention.

Take the AI Fatigue Quiz

issues.title: The Estimation Problem
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: Why your estimates got way too optimistic (and it is not a character flaw)
issues.body:
Three years ago, you estimated features in days and you were usually right within a factor of two.

Six months ago, you started using AI coding tools seriously. Your velocity went up. Your estimates went down. You thought: finally, I'm getting faster.

Then the sprint retrospectives started showing the pattern.

The tickets closed in half the estimated time. The code was shipped. The code reviews looked fine. And when something broke three weeks later, you had no idea why.

Your estimates did not get better. Your calibration got worse.

What actually happened to your estimation

Traditional estimation has a built-in accuracy mechanism: you know roughly how long things take because you have done them. You have debugged code you wrote. You have estimated features with your own hands. You have a felt sense of your unknown unknowns.

AI tooling quietly removes that mechanism.

When AI handles the implementation, debugging becomes faster. Edge cases are pre-handled by optimistic code. Unknown unknowns are hidden behind plausible output that looks correct in the review. You ship faster. The estimation looks better.

But the accuracy was never in the estimate. It was in the calibration — the relationship between what you knew and what you were estimating. That relationship has been severed.

The specific failure mode nobody names

There are two ways estimation degrades:

Burnout. You are exhausted, you are slower, your estimates go up. This is visible. This is fixable with rest.

AI-assisted estimation corruption. You are faster, your estimates go down, and you have lost the calibration that made them accurate. This is invisible from the outside. Your velocity metrics look great. Your retrospectives show short cycle times. Nobody flags it as a problem until something breaks in production and the on-call engineer realizes they cannot trace the failure back to anything in their mental model.

You are not worse at estimating. You have been cut off from the feedback loop that made your estimates accurate.

The test you can run right now

Go to your last 10 closed tickets. For each one, ask yourself:

  • Could I have estimated this without AI? Not: should I have. Could I have.
  • What would my estimate have been without AI? Was it shorter, longer, or the same as the actual?
  • If I had to estimate it again today without AI, what would I say?

Most engineers who run this test find something specific: their AI-assisted estimates are 30-50% shorter than their unaided estimates would be. Not because they got faster. Because they lost the parts of the problem that used to make estimation hard.

One practice that helps

Weekly unaided estimation sessions.

Once a week, before you open any AI tools, take one ticket from the backlog and estimate it by hand. Write down the number. Then open it up, use AI, build it, ship it. Compare your estimate to what actually happened.

Track the ratio: unaided estimate vs actual. Week over week, you will see whether your calibration is improving or degrading.

This is not about accuracy for its own sake. It is about staying connected to the relationship between your judgment and the work. That relationship is what makes you an engineer and not a code curator.

What 2,000+ engineers told us

71% of quiz takers said they could not explain most of the code they shipped last week.

44% said they are considering leaving tech entirely — not because of burnout, but because they have noticed the gap between what they ship and what they understand.

The estimation problem is not a character flaw. It is a structural feature of how AI tooling interacts with the feedback loop that makes engineering judgment accurate.

Where does your estimation currently fall? Take the 5-question AI Fatigue Quiz — it will tell you your specific profile and what to do about it.

Take the Quiz

Most-read on the site this week

The Estimation Problem — the full breakdown of why estimation accuracy degrades with AI tooling, the specific cognitive mechanisms, and the practice that rebuilds calibration.

12 Mental Models for Healthy AI Use — frameworks for staying in the learning loop while using AI tools. The Estimation Calibration Loop is one of the 12.

The Debugger Drift — the specific way debugging skill erodes differently from other skills, and why senior engineers feel it most acutely.

Forward this to an engineer friend who has been estimating strangely lately. They will not say it out loud but they know what you mean.

See you next Thursday.

— Sunny + The Clearing team

P.S. If you have been meaning to share your own AI fatigue story — we collect them at share-your-story.html. Anonymous. No pressure. It helps other engineers feel less alone.

issues.title: The Context Shell
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Context Shell — you know it, but you can't get to it
issues.body:
You know more about your codebase than you think. The problem is you can no longer access it without AI holding your hand.

This is what we are calling the context shell — the knowledge is not gone. It is inaccessible without an external scaffolding to retrieve it.

issues.title: The Prompting Reflex
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Prompting Reflex — your first instinct got replaced
issues.body:
Think about the last time you encountered a bug. Not a trivial one. A real one — something that required actually understanding what was happening.

What was your first move?

If you have been working with AI coding tools for 12 months or more, the honest answer is probably: you opened a chat and described the error.

That is not a workflow choice. That is a reflex. And it is worth examining carefully — because reflexes, unlike workflows, are not chosen. They are trained.

You used to have a different first instinct. You would read the error. You would open the relevant file. You would trace the stack. You might talk to a colleague. You would think about it in the shower, or on a walk, or while making coffee.

That instinct is not gone. It is just buried under a faster, more practiced response: prompt.

The reflex curve

When you learn any new skill — driving, touch-typing, a musical instrument — there is a period where it requires conscious effort. Then the neural pathway becomes automatic. The action becomes faster than conscious thought.

AI prompting has followed exactly this curve for most engineers. The conscious decision to open a chat has become a pre-conscious reflex in less than 18 months of regular use. That is unusually fast even for highly practiced skills.

Why this matters more than you think

Productivity is not the issue. Prompts produce useful output. That is not in dispute.

The issue is what the reflex is replacing.

The mental model of your codebase — the felt sense of how things fit together, where to look, what to expect — is built in the 20 minutes of deliberate attention before you reach for AI. The reflex skips that 20 minutes. It also skips the understanding that was built there.

Over time: the model stops growing. The reflex gets stronger. The gap between what you know and what the AI knows widens, and you stop noticing it.

A test you can run this week

For the next bug or problem you encounter — give yourself 20 minutes before you open any AI tool. Not to solve it without AI. Just to think about it first. Open the relevant files. Read the error. Trace the logic. Write down your hypothesis. Then open AI and compare: what you expected versus what it produces.

If the comparison surprises you more often than not, you are in the reflex stage. The AI is telling you things your mental model would have told you — if you still had the retrieval path to it.

The reflex is rebuildable. It just takes the opposite pattern: deliberate, consistent replacement of the shortcut with the slower path, enough times that the slower path becomes familiar again.

Take the AI Fatigue Quiz

issues.title: The Outside-In Problem
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Outside-In Problem — everything looks fine, something inside went quiet
issues.body: Everything on the outside looks fine. Your velocity is up. Your output is consistent. Your PRs are on time. And yet: something inside has gone quiet. This is the outside-in problem.

issues.title: The Competence Illusion
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: The Competence Illusion
issues.body:
Last week a senior engineer told us something that has been sitting with us since.

He said: "I passed every code review. I shipped every feature. And then I tried to do a take-home technical assessment for a new role — without AI — and I could not finish it."

This is the competence illusion. 71% of AI-assisted engineers feel like they ship code they could not write from scratch. 12% have a deliberate practice of building without AI tools.

The quiz tells you your specific profile on the capability-comfort spectrum.

issues.title: The Consistency Illusion
issues.author: Sunny + The Clearing team
issues.subject: Why showing up every day is not the same as getting better
issues.body:
A quiet letter for engineers who have been running in place.

You show up every day. You ship. You review the PRs. You answer the questions. You have been doing this for months.

And yet.

There is a specific kind of exhaustion that does not come from working hard. It comes from working hard and feeling like you are not getting anywhere. This feels like you are on a treadmill — moving fast, going nowhere.

This is the consistency illusion.

dispatch-41.title: The Competence Illusion
dispatch-41.description: Why AI-generated work feels like your work — and what that costs you over time. Surface comprehension vs. deep ownership, the 38% self-assessment failure, and four practices that help.

dispatch-42.title: The Attribution Problem
dispatch-42.description: When AI does the work, how do you show your manager what you actually contributed? A practical guide to being seen.
